title: From Glut to Guard: Oil Oversupply Meets New Energy Security Scramble
subhead: The US-Iran ceasefire has sent oil prices tumbling and unleashed a wave of trapped crude, but the real story is the lasting reshuffle — Asia building strategic reserves, Europe deploying warships, Ukraine striking Russian refineries 850 km deep, and OPEC facing a potential glut it didn't expect.

sections.heading: Hormuz: Open in Theory, Complicated in Practice
sections.body:
The US-Iran memorandum of understanding has technically reopened the Strait of Hormuz, but the physical reality is far messier. Bloomberg reports that at least eight tankers attempting to exit the Persian Gulf via the Omani coast made U-turns between Friday and Saturday, as disputes over which shipping lanes are safe persist. Iran has warned vessels against using routes it has not approved, creating a gap between the MoU's broad language and enforcement at sea.

Demining operations involving conventional minesweepers and underwater drones could take 40–50 days before insurers and shipping companies feel confident enough to resume normal traffic, according to the Saudi Gazette. In the meantime, more than 60 million barrels of trapped crude that were frozen when the conflict began are now flooding back into the market, according to Business Times Singapore, overwhelming demand.

France has deployed additional naval forces to the Persian Gulf to support a European-led Hormuz security mission, and the UK is advancing plans for a multinational coalition naval presence. Japan has not yet decided whether to contribute military forces. Iran's willingness to accept foreign naval patrols in its vicinity remains uncertain.
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between the diplomatic ceasefire and operational reality means the world's most critical oil chokepoint is only partially functional. Insurance costs, rerouting disputes, and demining timelines mean full normalization is weeks away — and any breakdown in negotiations could instantly reverse the fragile progress.

sections.heading: Oil Price Crash: From Famine to Flood in Weeks
sections.body:
Brent crude has erased all its wartime gains, tumbling 43% from a late-April high, according to Business Times Singapore. The physical oil market is now flashing weakness more extreme than any time since the COVID-19 demand collapse. The benchmark hit roughly $72 per barrel, according to Financial Express.

The reversal is staggering: less than three months ago, inventories were at critically low levels and industry executives warned of an inflationary energy crisis. Now, the simultaneous return of Iranian crude (with US sanctions waivers), Saudi and UAE exports at near-pre-war levels, and the release of 400 million barrels from the US Strategic Petroleum Reserve have created a supply wave with nowhere to go.

JPMorgan commodities chief Natasha Kaneva said: 'The market is facing the risk of a temporary glut as trapped oil finally re-enters a system that has already spent months learning how to function without it.' China, which slashed imports by some 5 million barrels per day during the crisis, has yet to meaningfully boost purchases, leaving Middle Eastern crude searching for buyers. Oman crude has sunk to a $4 discount to Dubai — the biggest since 2020.
sections.whyItMatters: The speed of the price collapse creates a new problem for OPEC: with Iranian barrels returning and US SPR releases still flowing, producers may face a glut and market-share fight just months after a supply crisis. The next OPEC+ meeting could pivot from 'how fast can we restore output' to 'how fast must we cut.'

sections.heading: Ukraine Strikes Russian Refineries 850 km Deep
sections.body:
Ukraine launched one of its largest drone attacks on St. Petersburg overnight on July 4, striking an oil terminal in Russia's second-largest city and the Baltic Sea port of Vysotsk, located roughly 170 km northwest. The strike was carried out by Ukraine's Special Operations Forces in coordination with the Unmanned Systems Forces and SBU, according to Firstpost. The target was 850 km from Ukraine's border.

The Vysotsk port handles oil, grain, coal, and LNG, making it a significant logistics node. Russian air defence claimed to have shot down 72 drones over the Leningrad region, but the oil terminal was still hit, with a large fire reported at the facility.

This continues a pattern of escalating Ukrainian drone strikes on Russian energy infrastructure that has created domestic fuel shortages across multiple Russian regions. In a remarkable reversal, Russia has begun importing gasoline from India — at least 60,000 metric tonnes already shipped, with plans to import 400,000 tonnes monthly from India and Belarus combined, according to Reuters and Republic World. The shortages have led to long queues at filling stations, fuel rationing, and record-high gasoline prices inside Russia.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia, one of the world's largest energy exporters, is now importing refined fuel from India — a country that buys most of its crude from Russia. This circular trade is a direct result of Ukraine's refinery-strike campaign and illustrates how drone warfare has shifted the energy calculus of the war.

sections.heading: G7 Tightens the Net on Russia's Shadow Fleet
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains, both the UK and Canada unveiled sweeping new sanctions targeting Russia's shadow oil fleet. The UK seized a Russian-linked vessel — the Smyrtos — in the English Channel, the first time British forces have physically captured a shadow fleet tanker. The vessel remains anchored off Dorset with its full crew still aboard, three weeks after seizure, according to MSN reporting.

Canada announced sanctions on 162 individuals, entities, and vessels linked to Russia's shadow fleet and energy sector, following a meeting between PM Carney and Zelenskyy. The UK's package also targets Russia's shadow fleet finance networks.

Meanwhile, a Russian warship fired warning shots at a yacht near it in the English Channel, escalating tensions in European waters days after the UK seizure, BBC reported. At the same summit, Trump signaled he may reimpose sanctions on Russian oil soon, as G7 leaders pushed Ukraine back onto the agenda, AP reported.
sections.whyItMatters: The coordinated G7 action against Russia's shadow fleet — combined with Trump's signal on Russian oil sanctions — represents a two-front squeeze: targeting the evasion infrastructure that keeps Russian oil flowing while also pressuring Moscow diplomatically. The Russian warning shots in the English Channel show the shadow fleet confrontation is becoming a live maritime security issue in European waters.

sections.heading: Asia's Energy Security Awakening: Reserves, Refineries, and Biofuels
sections.body:
The Iran war's disruption of Hormuz has triggered a continent-wide reassessment of energy security across Asia. India plans to build strategic reserves of crude, liquefied petroleum, and natural gas, according to Bloomberg. Indonesia has fast-tracked a diesel blend with 50% biofuel from palm plantations, while Vietnam is switching fully to ethanol-blended gasoline. Malaysia is also raising its biodiesel mandate.

Japan, which imports roughly 90% of its oil from the Persian Gulf, is considering a government-funded scheme to upgrade refinery units to process crude from a wider range of suppliers, Nikkei reported. Singapore and Thailand, both regular Middle Eastern LNG buyers, are now seeking long-term supply from the US. Pakistan and Vietnam are exploring domestic gas development and accelerated renewables.

South Korea and Indonesia have tightened oversight of speculative currency derivatives as the Iran war-driven oil disruption rattled forex markets, Nikkei reported. India's Finance Minister Sitharaman stated that India 'successfully managed the economic fallout' and remains the fastest-growing major economy.

ING's Warren Patterson noted: 'The Iran war has been a stark reminder that energy security remains one of Asia's biggest vulnerabilities. Resilience comes from a more diversified energy system.'
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz crisis didn't just cause short-term shortages — it's driving structural shifts in Asian energy policy that will reshape global trade flows for years. Diversification away from Middle Eastern dependence, new strategic reserves, and biofuel mandates are all accelerating. The question is whether these changes survive the return of cheap oil.

sections.heading: G7 Push on Critical Minerals: Takaichi's Stockpiling Initiative
sections.body:
Japanese PM Sanae Takaichi proposed a joint G7 critical minerals stockpiling initiative at the Evian summit, aimed at countering China's dominance in rare earth and battery supply chains. The proposal came as the Trump administration pushed a separate plan for a Western critical minerals trading bloc with regulated pricing, though Reuters reports that plan faces skepticism from G7 partners and divisions within the US mining industry over costs and a Pentagon AI-based pricing system.

Separately, Brazilian President Lula's adviser Celso Amorim told a Rio conference that the Global South must respond to the US-China AI race through BRICS, framing rare earths and data sovereignty as strategic priorities. India is also moving to reduce dependence on China for critical minerals, the Times of India reported.

ECB chief economist Philip Lane said the central bank will remain proactive on inflation despite falling energy prices following the Iran deal, signaling rates may stay elevated — a reminder that the energy shock's inflationary effects persist even as oil prices fall.
sections.whyItMatters: Two competing models for Western critical minerals coordination are now on the table — Japan's cooperative stockpiling approach and Trump's price-regulation bloc — but neither has full buy-in. Meanwhile, the Global South is organizing its own response. The minerals supply chain is becoming a three-way contest, not just a US-China duopoly.

sections.heading: European Aviation Fuel Still at 80% as War Toll Lingers
sections.body:
Despite the US-Iran ceasefire, European aviation fuel imports remain at just 80% of pre-war levels, according to Nikkei. High prices and potential flight reductions are expected to persist into autumn. The gap reflects the delayed recovery of refined product supply chains — even if crude oil is flowing again, the downstream infrastructure for jet fuel has not caught up.

The Bank of Japan raised interest rates to a 31-year high, citing energy-driven inflation as a key factor even as oil prices have fallen from wartime peaks. The BOJ's move signals that central banks are not yet convinced the energy shock has fully transmitted through the real economy. South Korea and Indonesia are also tightening derivatives oversight to defend their currencies against continued forex volatility linked to energy import costs.

The US used Iranian-style ship-to-ship transfer tactics to bypass its own Hormuz blockade and move crude out of the Persian Gulf during the conflict, Reuters reported — a remarkable illustration of how the disruption forced even the blockading power to adopt the evasion methods it typically polices.
sections.whyItMatters: The energy crisis is not over just because crude prices fell. Refined product shortages, central bank policy lag, and forex instability in import-dependent economies show the war's economic effects will outlast the ceasefire. The US using smuggling tactics against its own blockade underscores how fundamentally the Hormuz closure distorted global energy logistics.

bottomLine: The US-Iran ceasefire has turned oil from famine to flood: Brent has dropped 43% in weeks, 60 million barrels of trapped crude are re-entering a market that already learned to function without them, and OPEC may soon face a glut instead of a crisis. But the deeper story is structural — Asia is building strategic reserves and diversifying away from Middle Eastern dependence, Ukraine's drone strikes have forced Russia to import Indian gasoline, the G7 is tightening the shadow fleet net while Trump signals new Russian oil sanctions, and Hormuz is only partially operational with 40-50 days of demining ahead. The war may be winding down, but the energy system it reshaped is not going back to what it was.
sources: Reuters, Bloomberg, Business Times Singapore, Financial Express, Nikkei Asia, Saudi Gazette, BBC, AP News, Firstpost, Republic World, Outlook India, Taipei Times, OilPrice.com, MSN, ING, JPMorgan, Al-Monitor, NPR, The Economist, Times of India, Kyiv Independent, Indian Express
